sections.title: The Myth of Icarus
sections.instructions: Read the myth of Icarus, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
One morning on the island of Crete, Icarus and his father Daedalus were in their workshop. Daedalus was an inventor. Suddenly, King Minos arrived and he spoke to Daedalus.

'I'd like you to build me a labyrinth,' he said. 'I want to put the Minotaur inside it.' The Minotaur was a terrible monster. He had the head of a bull and the body of a man.

King Minos was pleased when he saw Daedalus's work. He put the Minotaur inside the labyrinth. Then he locked Daedalus and Icarus inside a tower. 'What are you doing?' said Daedalus. 'Well,' said the King, 'you know the secret of how to get out of the labyrinth. And I don't want anyone else to know it.'

Daedalus and his son felt sad. Days passed, then Daedalus had an idea. He collected feathers from the birds that flew to the window of the tower. Then he made wings from a candle to stick the feathers together.

Daedalus told Icarus what to do. 'Follow me. Don't go too high because the sun will melt the wax in your wings. Don't go too low because the sea will make the feathers in your wings wet. Are you ready? Go!' Daedalus and Icarus jumped out of the window. They flew away from the island, and away from King Minos.

Daedalus flew in front. Icarus followed behind. Icarus loved flying. He was just like a bird! He forgot his father's words and he flew higher and higher. But as he got nearer to the sun, the wax between the feathers began to melt.

When Daedalus looked behind him, he couldn't see his son. 'Icarus!' he shouted. 'Icarus! Where are you?' Daedalus looked down. There were feathers in the sea. Daedalus went to the nearest island. He sat and looked at the sea for a long time, and he felt sad for his son.

sections.title: A Theatre Workshop
sections.instructions: Read the text about the theatre workshop, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
In our drama group, we use different materials to make our costumes, props and sets. We use materials with different properties. Some materials are flexible and light. Some materials are rigid and strong. It's important to use materials that are safe.

We make masks with papier-mâché. It's flexible and light because it is made of paper, glue and water. You can paint the masks and decorate them with lots of different things.

Today we're making Greek masks. Theatre was very important in Ancient Greece. Most cities had a theatre and people went there to watch comedies (funny shows) or tragedies (sad or serious shows). The actors used masks to show different emotions more clearly. Some masks were double-sided, which means they could have one face on one side and a different one on the other side. Let's make happy and sad double-sided masks!

To make a mask, you need newspaper cut into strips. Mix some white glue in a bowl with water. Put the paper strips one by one into the bowl and cover them with glue. Put the strips on a plastic shape, like a balloon. When it's dry, paint it and decorate it.

options: A plastic shape like a balloon
questions.answer: A plastic shape like a balloon

sections.title: Cappadocia
sections.instructions: Read the text about Cappadocia, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
Cappadocia, Turkey, is different from any other place on Earth. It is a very high area: over 1,000 metres above the sea. There are many mountains across this area which are thousands of years old and made from volcanic rock. Some of these mountains are really high. Mt Kayseri, which is the highest, is 3,916 metres!

Because Cappadocia is so high, the weather can change a lot. It can be very hot some days and very cold other days.

The people who lived in Cappadocia many years ago made small houses inside the mountains and rocks. You should visit it because you can explore inside the mountains and see how the people lived!

questions.answer: To explore the mountains and see how people lived

sections.title: The Project
sections.instructions: Read the story about Katy, Mike and Harold, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
Katy, Mike and Harold were in the library talking about a project about Turkey. Mr Carlton, their teacher, said they could choose any topic for their project, but it should be about Turkey.

'Betty's group is doing their project about the geography of Turkey,' said Katy.

Harold was reading about Turkey on a website. 'The history of Turkey is very interesting. It was part of the Ottoman Empire!'

'Freddy's group's doing their project about that,' said Katy. 'What about science?'

'No,' said Harold. He didn't like science. 'The project's for Friday,' she said. 'Let's hurry up!'

Katy looked at her timetable. 'I'm sure that Turkey has lots of stories and myths. These can teach us a lot about a place. Our project can be about Turkish literature.'

'Great idea! We should find one on the Internet. Mr Carlton likes us to use IT,' said Mike.

'Why don't we do something original, like re-write the story as a poem? I'm good at writing poems,' said Katy. 'Mike's good at art. He can draw the pictures. And you're good at acting, Harold. You can read it to the class!'

'Perfect,' said Mike. So that's what they did!

sections.title: The Great Barrier Reef
sections.instructions: Read the text about the Great Barrier Reef, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
The Great Barrier Reef is in the sea near Australia. It is the largest coral reef in the world. It is so big that you can see it from space! The reef is about 2,300 kilometres long.

The reef is home to many different animals. There are over 1,500 types of fish, 400 types of coral and many sea turtles, dolphins and sharks. The water is warm and very clear, so you can see the colourful fish and coral under the water.

Many people visit the Great Barrier Reef every year. They go snorkelling and diving to see the beautiful animals and plants under the sea. Some people take boat trips to see the reef from above.

But the Great Barrier Reef is in danger. The water is getting warmer because of climate change. When the water is too warm, the coral turns white and dies. Scientists are working hard to protect the reef and keep it healthy for the future.

questions.answer: The coral turns white and dies

sections.title: Sports Day
sections.instructions: Read the story about Sports Day at Greenfield School, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
Last Friday was Sports Day at Greenfield School. All the students were very excited. There were many different events: running, jumping, throwing and team races.

Emma was in the 100-metre race. She practised every day after school for two weeks. On Sports Day, she ran as fast as she could and came second! She was very happy because last year she came fifth.

Tom was good at throwing. He threw the ball 25 metres in the javelin event. That was the best throw of the day! His teacher, Mrs Davies, gave him a gold medal.

The best part of the day was the team relay race. Each team had four runners. Emma's team, the Blue Team, was winning, but then Jake dropped the baton. The Red Team won the race in the end.

After all the events, everyone had ice cream and lemonade. The headteacher, Mr Patel, said it was the best Sports Day ever. Everyone agreed!

sections.title: Iceland
sections.instructions: Read the text about Iceland, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
Iceland is a small country in the north of Europe. It is an island in the Atlantic Ocean. About 370,000 people live there. The capital city is Reykjavik.

Iceland is famous for its volcanoes. There are over 30 active volcanoes on the island. Sometimes the volcanoes erupt and hot lava comes out. Iceland also has many geysers. A geyser is a hole in the ground where hot water shoots up into the air. The most famous geyser in Iceland is called Strokkur. It erupts every 6 to 10 minutes!

In winter, the days in Iceland are very short. In December, there are only about 4 hours of daylight. But you can see the Northern Lights — beautiful green, purple and pink lights that dance across the sky at night.

In summer, it is the opposite. The sun almost never goes down! In June, it is light for almost 24 hours. People call this the 'midnight sun.'

questions.answer: A hole where hot water shoots up

sections.title: The School Trip
sections.instructions: Read the story about the school trip to the museum, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
On Wednesday, Class 4B went on a school trip to the Natural History Museum. Their teacher, Miss Wilson, took them on the bus. The journey took about 45 minutes.

When they arrived, a museum guide called Anna showed them around. First, they went to the dinosaur room. There was a huge skeleton of a Tyrannosaurus Rex. It was 12 metres long! Jack said it was the best thing he had ever seen.

Next, they visited the room about oceans. They learned about whales, sharks and jellyfish. There was a model of a blue whale hanging from the ceiling. Lily thought it was amazing.

After lunch, they went to the rock and mineral room. They saw diamonds, gold and many colourful crystals. The guide told them that some rocks are millions of years old.

Before they left, everyone visited the gift shop. Jack bought a small model dinosaur and Lily bought a book about ocean animals. On the bus home, Miss Wilson asked them what they had learned. Everyone had a favourite fact to share!

questions.answer: A model of a blue whale

sections.title: The Altamira Caves
sections.instructions: Read the text about the Altamira Caves, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
Paula's dad is an archaeologist. Last year, he visited Spain because there is lots of work for an archaeologist in Spain. It's got many examples of how people lived in the past. His exciting new job was to study the Altamira Caves.

The caves are famous because archaeologists discovered the first cave paintings there in 1880. We did not know that in the past, people told stories using art. They didn't write like we do today.

The paintings are over 16,000 years old. Many of the paintings on the cave walls show animals and people hunting. In one cave, a really big painting showed a herd of bison. We think the paintings tell the story of how people hunted.

There were no paints or paintbrushes then, so the cave people used the things they found around them. They used sticks, bones and even their hands. They made paint out of natural materials like rocks, earth and animal fat.

Paula says: 'When I grow up, I want to be an archaeologist like my father. He tells me all about his work, and I think it is amazing!'

sections.title: Don Quixote
sections.instructions: Read the play script about Don Quixote, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
A long time ago in Spain, in a land called La Mancha, there was a quiet village with a large house. The man who lived in the house was called Alonso Quixano. Alonso read books all day about brave knights. He read so much that sometimes he forgot to eat or sleep. He dreamed about saving women in danger and fighting dragons.

One day he decided to become a knight and he changed his name to Don Quixote. He put on his grandfather's armour and he rode his horse Rocinante. He asked his good friend Sancho Panza to join him and he promised to pay him lots of money in return.

Don Quixote said: 'Look, Sancho! Our next great adventure. Can you see them?'
Sancho said: 'What?'
Don Quixote said: 'Thirty or forty giants over there.'
Sancho said: 'What giants?'
Don Quixote said: 'Over there! Look how long their arms are. They're moving in all directions.'
Sancho said: 'Dear friend, you think they look like giants but they're windmills and the arms you can see are their sails blowing in the wind.'

But Don Quixote rode towards the windmills. The windmill caught Don Quixote and Rocinante and threw them down.

Sancho said: 'Are you alright? I told you that...'
Don Quixote said: 'Be quiet, Sancho! Someone changed the giants into windmills. It's magic!'
Sancho said: 'Yes, my friend. Let me help you.'

And they continued on their journey to find their next adventure.

questions.answer: Someone changed them into windmills by magic
